notes.concept: The plasma membrane is selectively permeable and encloses cell contents.
notes.fact: Fluid mosaic model: phospholipid bilayer with proteins embedded.
notes.tip: Think: boundary + transport + signaling.

notes.concept: Nucleus controls cellular activities through genetic material.
notes.fact: Nuclear envelope has pores for exchange of materials.
notes.tip: Nucleolus is the site of rRNA synthesis.

notes.concept: Ribosomes are non-membranous particles for protein synthesis.
notes.fact: 80S ribosomes occur in eukaryotic cytoplasm; 70S in prokaryotes and organelles.
notes.tip: Svedberg units are not additive.

notes.concept: Endoplasmic reticulum forms a membrane network in cytoplasm.
notes.fact: RER helps protein synthesis; SER helps lipid synthesis and detoxification.
notes.tip: Rough = ribosomes present.

notes.concept: Golgi apparatus modifies, packages, and secretes biomolecules.
notes.fact: Cisternae are flattened sacs arranged in stacks.
notes.tip: Golgi is central to secretion in eukaryotic cells.

notes.concept: Mitochondria are sites of aerobic respiration.
notes.fact: Inner membrane forms cristae and encloses matrix containing enzymes.
notes.tip: Mitochondria have their own DNA and ribosomes.

notes.concept: Plastids are found in plant cells and carry pigments or food reserves.
notes.fact: Chloroplasts have thylakoids arranged as grana.
notes.tip: Leucoplasts are colorless storage plastids.

notes.concept: Cell cycle includes interphase and M phase.
notes.fact: Mitosis proceeds as prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase.
notes.tip: PMAT is a high-yield sequence for revision.

quiz.question: According to the fluid mosaic model, the plasma membrane is primarily made of:
quiz.explanation: NCERT describes membrane as a phospholipid bilayer with proteins embedded in it.

quiz.question: Nucleolus is mainly associated with:
quiz.explanation: Nucleolus is the site of rRNA synthesis and assembly of ribosomal subunits.

quiz.question: Ribosomes in eukaryotic cytoplasm are:
quiz.explanation: Cytoplasmic ribosomes in eukaryotes are 80S.

quiz.question: Rough endoplasmic reticulum is directly linked with:
quiz.explanation: RER has ribosomes and assists in protein synthesis.

quiz.question: Golgi apparatus is best known for:
quiz.explanation: Golgi modifies, packages, and dispatches cellular products.

quiz.question: Cristae are found in:
quiz.explanation: Cristae are infoldings of the inner mitochondrial membrane.

quiz.question: Pigments for photosynthesis are present in:
quiz.explanation: Chloroplast contains chlorophyll and related pigments.

quiz.question: The correct sequence of mitotic phases is:
quiz.explanation: NCERT sequence for mitosis is PMAT.

quiz.question: Cell wall in plants is mainly made of:
quiz.explanation: Primary plant cell wall is mainly cellulose with other components.

quiz.question: Svedberg unit is related to:
quiz.explanation: Svedberg value indicates sedimentation behavior, not direct additive mass.

notes.concept: A chemical bond forms by transfer or sharing of valence electrons.
notes.fact: Ionic bond forms by electron transfer; covalent bond by sharing.
notes.tip: Check valence shell configuration first.

notes.concept: Octet rule explains stability for many main-group compounds.
notes.fact: Atoms tend to attain noble gas configuration in bonding.
notes.tip: Remember exceptions like incomplete octet and expanded octet.

notes.concept: Lewis structures represent valence electrons as dots.
notes.fact: Total valence electron count is the first step in Lewis structure.
notes.tip: Place least electronegative atom at center (except H).

notes.concept: VSEPR predicts molecular shape from electron pair repulsion.
notes.fact: Lone pair-lone pair repulsion is strongest.
notes.tip: Repulsion order: LP-LP > LP-BP > BP-BP.

notes.concept: Hybridization mixes orbitals to form equivalent hybrid orbitals.
notes.fact: sp, sp2, sp3 correspond to linear, trigonal planar, tetrahedral electron geometry.
notes.tip: Steric number helps identify hybridization quickly.

notes.concept: Sigma bonds form by head-on overlap; pi bonds by sidewise overlap.
notes.fact: Single bond = one sigma; double bond = one sigma + one pi.
notes.tip: All multiple bonds contain exactly one sigma bond.

notes.concept: Bond polarity depends on electronegativity difference.
notes.fact: Dipole moment is a vector quantity.
notes.tip: Molecular symmetry can cancel bond dipoles.

notes.concept: Hydrogen bond is a strong intermolecular force in specific compounds.
notes.fact: Occurs when H is bonded to N, O, or F and interacts with lone pair.
notes.tip: Hydrogen bonding raises boiling points in many molecules.

quiz.question: Ionic bond is formed by:
quiz.explanation: Ionic bond involves transfer of electrons and electrostatic attraction.

quiz.question: Central atom in Lewis structures is usually:
quiz.explanation: General NCERT guideline places less electronegative atom at center.

quiz.question: Repulsion strength in VSEPR follows:
quiz.explanation: Lone pairs occupy more space and repel more strongly.

quiz.question: Hybridization for methane (CH4) carbon is:
quiz.explanation: CH4 has tetrahedral arrangement with sp3 hybridized carbon.

quiz.question: A double bond consists of:
quiz.explanation: One sigma bond forms first; additional overlap gives one pi bond.

quiz.question: Dipole moment is:
quiz.explanation: Dipole moment has magnitude and direction.

quiz.question: Hydrogen bond is strongest when hydrogen is attached to:
quiz.explanation: High electronegativity and small size of N/O/F favor H-bonding.

quiz.question: Molecular shape of CO2 is:
quiz.explanation: Central carbon has two electron domains and no lone pair, giving linear shape.

quiz.question: Among NH3 and BF3, the molecule with incomplete octet at central atom is:
quiz.explanation: Boron in BF3 has only six electrons around it.

quiz.question: In H2O, the electron pair geometry around oxygen is:
quiz.explanation: Two bond pairs and two lone pairs correspond to tetrahedral electron pair geometry.

notes.concept: First law defines inertia and frames Newtonian mechanics.
notes.fact: A body remains at rest or in uniform straight-line motion unless acted upon by external unbalanced force.
notes.tip: Net external force zero means no acceleration.

notes.concept: Second law links force with rate of change of momentum.
notes.fact: For constant mass, F = ma.
notes.tip: Use vector form for direction-sensitive problems.

notes.concept: Third law states forces occur in equal and opposite pairs.
notes.fact: Action and reaction act on different bodies.
notes.tip: They do not cancel each other because bodies differ.

notes.concept: Linear momentum is product of mass and velocity.
notes.fact: Momentum is conserved in an isolated system.
notes.tip: Use conservation when external impulse is negligible.

notes.concept: Friction opposes relative motion or tendency of motion.
notes.fact: Maximum static friction is fs(max) = mu_s N.
notes.tip: Static friction self-adjusts up to its maximum value.

notes.concept: Kinetic friction acts when surfaces are sliding.
notes.fact: fk = mu_k N and typically mu_k < mu_s.
notes.tip: Direction is opposite to relative sliding velocity.

notes.concept: Pseudo force appears in non-inertial frames.
notes.fact: Magnitude equals m*a_frame and acts opposite frame acceleration.
notes.tip: Add pseudo force only in accelerated reference frames.

notes.concept: Circular motion requires centripetal force toward center.
notes.fact: Centripetal force magnitude is mv^2/r.
notes.tip: This is net radial force, not a new independent force type.

quiz.question: Newton's first law is also called law of:
quiz.explanation: It expresses the tendency of bodies to resist change in state of motion.

quiz.question: For constant mass, Newton's second law gives:
quiz.explanation: Rate of change of momentum reduces to ma for constant mass.

quiz.question: Action and reaction forces:
options: Always cancel each other on one body
quiz.explanation: Third-law pair acts on two interacting bodies.

quiz.question: Unit of linear momentum is:
quiz.explanation: Momentum p = mv has SI unit kg m s^-1.

quiz.question: Maximum static friction is given by:
quiz.explanation: At limiting equilibrium, static friction reaches mu_s N.

quiz.question: Kinetic friction coefficient compared to static friction coefficient is usually:
quiz.explanation: Typically mu_k < mu_s for same surfaces.

quiz.question: Pseudo force in an accelerating frame acts:
quiz.explanation: Pseudo force is m*a_frame opposite to frame acceleration.

quiz.question: Centripetal force direction is:
quiz.explanation: It is radial and directed inward.

quiz.question: In isolated systems, total momentum:
quiz.explanation: Conservation of momentum holds when net external force is zero.

quiz.question: If net external force is zero, acceleration is:
quiz.explanation: From F = ma, zero net force implies zero acceleration.

notes.concept: Breathing includes inspiration and expiration; respiration is cellular oxidation.
notes.fact: Breathing is a physical process, while respiration is biochemical.
notes.tip: Differentiate ventilation from cellular respiration in MCQs.

notes.concept: Human respiratory tract ends in alveoli for gas exchange.
notes.fact: Alveolar walls are thin and richly supplied with capillaries.
notes.tip: Large surface area supports rapid diffusion.

notes.concept: Oxygen transport in blood is mainly through hemoglobin.
notes.fact: About 97% O2 is carried as oxyhemoglobin in RBCs.
notes.tip: A small fraction dissolves directly in plasma.

notes.concept: CO2 transport occurs in three forms in blood.
notes.fact: Major fraction is transported as bicarbonate ions.
notes.tip: Carbonic anhydrase in RBCs speeds conversion.

notes.concept: Inspiration occurs when diaphragm contracts and flattens.
notes.fact: Thoracic volume increases and intrapulmonary pressure falls.
notes.tip: Air moves from higher to lower pressure.

notes.concept: Expiration at rest is mostly passive.
notes.fact: Relaxation of diaphragm and external intercostals reduces thoracic volume.
notes.tip: Forced expiration recruits abdominal muscles.

notes.concept: Respiratory pigments increase oxygen carrying capacity.
notes.fact: Hemoglobin has high affinity for oxygen in lungs and releases in tissues.
notes.tip: Affinity shifts with pO2, pCO2, and pH (Bohr effect context).

notes.concept: Neural regulation of breathing centers in medulla and pons.
notes.fact: Chemoreceptors monitor CO2 and H+ changes to regulate ventilation.
notes.tip: Rise in CO2 strongly stimulates respiration.

quiz.question: Site of gaseous exchange in human lungs is:
quiz.explanation: Alveoli provide thin respiratory surface for diffusion.

quiz.question: Major transport form of oxygen in blood is:
quiz.explanation: Most oxygen binds to hemoglobin in RBCs.

quiz.question: Most carbon dioxide is transported as:
quiz.explanation: Majority of CO2 is carried in bicarbonate form.

quiz.question: During inspiration, diaphragm:
quiz.explanation: Contraction flattens diaphragm, increasing thoracic volume.

quiz.question: Normal quiet expiration is generally:
quiz.explanation: It mostly occurs due to elastic recoil and muscle relaxation.

quiz.question: Respiratory rhythm in humans is primarily regulated by:
quiz.explanation: Medullary respiratory centers set basic rhythm.

quiz.question: Increase in blood CO2 usually causes breathing rate to:
quiz.explanation: CO2 rise stimulates chemoreceptors and increases ventilation.

quiz.question: External intercostal muscles aid in:
quiz.explanation: Their contraction elevates ribs and expands thorax in inspiration.

quiz.question: Breathing differs from cellular respiration because breathing is:
quiz.explanation: Breathing is mechanical ventilation; respiration is biochemical oxidation.

quiz.question: Gas diffusion across alveolar membrane depends directly on:
quiz.explanation: Diffusion follows partial pressure gradients across the membrane.
